model.label: Claude Opus 4.6
model.description: Anthropic • Hybrid reasoning model that pushes the frontier for coding and AI agents, featuring a 1M context window.

pick.rationale: Michigan is the #2 ranked team nationally with a staggering +38.38 adjusted efficiency margin, roughly 12.7 points per 100 possessions higher than Alabama's +25.64, driven by the nation's best defense (AdjD 88.4) that is specifically built to neutralize even elite offenses like Alabama's 3rd-ranked attack (AdjO 129.0). Alabama's critical vulnerability is its 67th-ranked defense (AdjD 103.4), which will struggle to contain Michigan's top-8 offense, while the Crimson Tide are further weakened by the indefinite suspension of second-leading scorer and top three-point shooter Aden Holloway following a felony arrest. The roughly 15-point defensive efficiency gap between these teams is the decisive factor, as Michigan's suffocating defense should limit Alabama's offensive output while the Wolverines' balanced attack exploits Alabama's porous defense to build a comfortable margin in this 1-vs-4 Sweet 16 matchup.

reasoningStep.title: Michigan vs Alabama — Midwest Sweet 16 1
reasoningStep.summary: Michigan's historically elite defense and massive efficiency margin advantage make them a strong favorite over a diminished Alabama team missing its top three-point shooter.
evidence: Michigan ranks #2 nationally in both KenPom (AdjEM +38.38) and Torvik with the nation's best defense (AdjD 88.4)
Alabama ranks #16 in KenPom (AdjEM +25.64) with a vulnerable 67th-ranked defense (AdjD 103.4)
The ~12.7-point efficiency margin gap per 100 possessions is enormous for a Sweet 16 matchup
Alabama is without suspended guard Aden Holloway (felony arrest), their second-leading scorer and top three-point shooter
Michigan's Yaxel Lendeborg confirmed he expects to play despite Big Ten Championship ankle injury
Alabama's 23-9 record with an early SEC Tournament exit reflects inconsistency compared to Michigan's dominant 31-2 campaign
Michigan's offense (AdjO 126.7, 8th nationally) should exploit Alabama's below-average defense

result.text: Can Alabama basketball survive March Madness without Aden Holloway? Skip to main content Home TuscPreps College Columns TideSports.com SportsbookWire MMA Junkie Golf Week What does Alabama basketball look like without Aden Holloway in March Madness? Emilee Smarr Tuscaloosa News Spirits were low for Alabama basketball coming into the NCAA Tournament after an early exit from the SEC tourney in Nashville. Now, the Crimson Tide carries more heartbreak to Tampa as it stares down March Madness without its top three-point shooter. On Monday, March 16, Alabama guard Aden Holloway was arrested for first-degree possession of marijuana and failure to affix a tax stamp and was transported to the Tuscaloosa County Jail. The University of Alabama has yet to release a statement on Holloway, or his status. However, facing two felony charges, it is hard to see a world in which the junior returns to competition. Not with the Crimson Tide, but possibly ever again. REQUIRED READING: What failure to affix tax stamp means for Alabama basketball's Aden Holloway GOODBREAD: Why Alabama's Nate Oats should suspend Aden Holloway Holloway's decisions have impacted more than just himself, though. The question is no longer whether Alabama can win a national championship in the face of adversity. Instead, it's if the Crimson Tide can survive even one game without one of the anchors to its nationally-acclaimed backcourt. Prior to his arrest, Holloway was averaging 16.8 points per game. Converting nearly 44% of attempts, he was the nation's 11th best shooter from the arc and ranked third in the SEC. He shot 48% from the field and also had 3.8 assists per game. Alabama has competed without Holloway before — as he dealt with a nagging wrist injury throughout non-conference play. Considering the Crimson Tide's strength-of-schedule, the fact that Alabama has done it without Holloway at 100% could suggest that it isn't time to panic. Well, March is not December. Holloway missed the season opener against North Dakota. He also was sidelined for the SEC/ACC Challenge against Clemson and the next game against UTSA. Arizona marked the Auburn transfer's return, but the 96-75 loss also gave an idea of what Alabama looks like on a Holloway off-day as he went scoreless over 21 minutes. A complete domination of UTSA also made it easier to forget how the Crimson Tide crumbled in the second half against the Tigers. Holloway hasn't missed a game since Dec. 13, and Alabama couldn't have afforded for him to be on the bench against Texas A&M or on the road at Neville Arena. In those games, the 6-foot-1 downhill threat delivered critical buckets late. As of late, he's also emerged as a key rebounder and defender for the Crimson Tide. With or without Holloway, defense has been a problem. Offensively, Alabama doesn't have the depth from the perimeter that Crimson Tide coach Nate Oats had hoped for in the preseason. Alabama finds more ways to score around the rim and from the one-line nowadays. Still, th

Holloway missed the season opener against North Dakota. He also was sidelined for the SEC/ACC Challenge against Clemson and the next game against UTSA. Arizona marked the Auburn transfer's return, but the 96-75 loss also gave an idea of what Alabama looks like on a Holloway off-day as he went scoreless over 21 minutes. A complete domination of UTSA also made it easier to forget how the Crimson Tide crumbled in the second half against the Tigers. Holloway hasn't missed a game since Dec. 13, and Alabama couldn't have afforded for him to be on the bench against Texas A&M or on the road at Neville Arena. In those games, the 6-foot-1 downhill threat delivered critical buckets late. As of late, he's also emerged as a key rebounder and defender for the Crimson Tide. With or without Holloway, defense has been a problem. Offensively, Alabama doesn't have the depth from the perimeter that Crimson Tide coach Nate Oats had hoped for in the preseason. Alabama finds more ways to score around the rim and from the one-line nowadays. Still, th

result.text: Yaxel Lendeborg leaves no NCAA Tournament doubt after ankle injury vs. Purdue Skip to main content About Michigan Wolverines News Michigan Football Michigan Basketball FanSided NCAA Sites Follow Us Share on X About Michigan Wolverines News Michigan Football Michigan Football Michigan Football All-Time Lists Michigan Football Recruiting Michigan Basketball Michigan Basketball Michigan Basketball All-Time Lists Michigan Basketball Recruiting Michigan Women's Basketball FanSided NCAA Sites Share on X Yaxel Lendeborg leaves no NCAA Tournament doubt after ankle injury vs. Purdue Yaxel Lendeborg made it abundantly clear if his injury is going to keep him out of March Madness. By John Buhler | Share on Facebook Share on X Share on Reddit Copy Article Add us as a preferred source Yaxel Lendeborg, Michigan Wolverines | Junfu Han / USA TODAY NETWORK via Imagn Images The Michigan Wolverines may have earned a No. 1 seed in the NCAA Tournament, but it did not go according to plan for them in the Big Ten Championship. Not only did they fall in the conference title bout to Purdue, but star forward Yaxel Lendeborg suffered a scary ankle injury . Michigan has a loaded roster, but he has been the guy Dusty May has gone to all season long to carry the team in the clutch. --> The Michigan Wolverines may have earned a No. 1 seed in the NCAA Tournament, but it did not go according to plan for them in the Big Ten Championship. Not only did they fall in the conference title bout to Purdue, but star forward Yaxel Lendeborg suffered a scary ankle injury . Michigan has a loaded roster, but he has been the guy Dusty May has gone to all season long to carry the team in the clutch. Thankfully, it does not look like this ankle injury will be keeping him out of the NCAA Tournament... --> Thankfully, it does not look like this ankle injury will be keeping him out of the NCAA Tournament... Michigan forward Yaxel Lendeborg told me he has a low ankle-sprain and expects to play in the Wolverines’ NCAA Tournament opener. — Alejandro Zúñiga (@ByAZuniga) March 15, 2026 Michigan earned the No. 1 seed in the Midwest Region. The Wolverines will take on the the winner of the UMBC Retrievers and the Howard Bison in their First Four game to determine the No. 16 seed. Frankly, Lendeborg does not need to play very much at all in that first-round game. However he will need to be called upon in the Round of 32 game between either No. 8 Georgia or No. 9 Saint Louis. --> Michigan earned the No. 1 seed in the Midwest Region. The Wolverines will take on the the winner of the UMBC Retrievers and the Howard Bison in their First Four game to determine the No. 16 seed. Frankly, Lendeborg does not need to play very much at all in that first-round game. However he will need to be called upon in the Round of 32 game between either No. 8 Georgia or No. 9 Saint Louis. Michigan should be able to get to the Sweet 16 quite comfortably, but injuries can always take a toll. --> Michigan should be able to get

Now I have a comprehensive picture of this matchup. Let me synthesize the analysis:

**Michigan (1-seed):**
- KenPom #2: AdjO 126.7 (8th), AdjD 88.4 (best in nation), AdjEM +38.38
- Torvik #2: AdjO 127.7 (7th), AdjD 90.5 (2nd), Record 31-2
- Yaxel Lendeborg (ankle) expected to play — confirmed he intends to play despite Big Ten Championship injury

**Alabama (4-seed):**
- KenPom #16: AdjO 129.0 (3rd), AdjD 103.4, AdjEM +25.64
- Torvik #17: AdjO 129.4 (3rd), AdjD 102.8 (67th), Record 23-9
- Missing Aden Holloway (suspended, felony arrest) — second-leading scorer and top 3-point shooter
- Early SEC Tournament exit

The efficiency margin gap is enormous: +38.38 vs +25.64 = ~12.7 points per 100 possessions. Michigan's historically elite defense (AdjD 88.4) is the key matchup factor against Alabama's 3rd-ranked offense. Alabama's defense (103.4) is a major vulnerability — ranked 67th — against Michigan's top-8 offense.

Alabama's critical vulnerability is its 67th-ranked defense (AdjD 103.4), which will struggle to contain Michigan's top-8 offense, while the Crimson Tide are further weakened by the indefinite suspension of second-leading scorer and top three-point shooter Aden Holloway following a felony arrest. The roughly 15-point defensive efficiency gap between these teams (88.4 vs 103.4) is the decisive factor, as Michigan's suffocating defense should limit Alabama's offensive output while the Wolverines' own balanced attack exploits Alabama's porous defense to build a comfortable margin in this 1-vs-4 Sweet 16 matchup."

The ~12.7-point efficiency margin gap is enormous for a Sweet 16 matchup.
